coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots induce in-situ formation of oxygen vacancies and domination of {001} facets in BiOBr microflower for simultaneous removal of aqueous tetracycline and hexavalent chromium
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Chaoqun
Zhao, Ziqing
Wang, Xingyue
Li, MingYu
Jiang, Jingjing
Dong, Shuangshi
coredata.dc:description: Crystal facet engineering and defect engineering are promising strategies to enhance the photocatalytic performance of BiOBr, but limited work has been reported to realize the simultaneous exposure of highly active facets and the introduction of oxygen vacancies by a single approach. In this work, carbon quantum dots-modified bismuth oxybromide (CQDs/BiOBr) with {001} exposed facets and surface oxygen vacancies was synthesized by a two-step hydrothermal method. The characterization and density functional theory calculation results verified that the introduction of CQDs promoted the growth of BiOBr with highly exposed {001} crystal facets and induced the in situ formation of oxygen vacancies by extracting O atoms from the [Bi2O2]2+ layer. The dual functionality of the CQDs allowed them to simultaneously remove tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) and Cr(VI) under visible light irradiation. The photocatalytic reaction rate constants in the coexisting pollutant system were 3.71 (TCH) and 13.6-times (Cr(VI)) higher than those of the mono-pollutant systems. This work provides a candidate method to design highly efficient catalysts for environmental remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Oxygen vacancies
{001} exposed facet
Toxicity

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic reaction under single TCH or Cr(VI) system
Photocatalytic reaction under coexistence of TCH + Cr(VI)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern of the as-prepared CQDs(x)/BiOBr photocatalysts × (x = 0, 0.5%, 1%, 1.5%, 2.5%.); (b) ESR signals of BiOBr and CQDs (x)/BiOBr; (c) calculated Bi–O bond length, Surface Energy and OV Formation Energy for {001} facet of y/CQDs/BiOBr(y = 1, 2, 3, 4. y means qualitative content of CQDs); (d) Computational model of the CQDs/BiOBr{001} interface.
XPS spectra of CQDs (1.5%)/BiOBr and BiOBr: (a) survey, (b) Bi 4f, (c) O 1 s, (d) Br 3d, (e) C 1 s; (f) FT-IR spectra of CQDs (1.5%)/BiOBr and BiOBr.
SEM images of (a) BiOBr, the as-prepared CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr of (b) SEM, (c) TEM and HETEM images (d) SAED images.

(a) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the photocatalysts; inset is (αhν) 1/2 vs hν curves; (b) The photoluminescence spectra of BiOBr and CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr with excitation wavelength at 380 nm; (c) Transient photocurrent responses of different samples (d) EIS changes of photocatalysts.

(a) Photocatalytic removal of TCH or Cr(VI) over CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr under visible light irradiation in the presence of different scavengers, **P < 0.01; *P < 0.05; (b) ESR spectra of different samples in DMPO under visible light irradiation.

Photocatalytic activities of the as-prepared photocatalysts for (a) degradation of TCH, (b)reduction of Cr(VI) under single system at unadjusted pH = 5.4; (c) degradation of TCH, (d) reduction of Cr(VI) under bi-pollutant coexistence system at unadjusted pH = 5.4; (e) degradation of TCH, (f) reduction of Cr(VI) under coexistence system at adjusted pH = 3.

(a) cycling removal of TCH and Cr(VI) simultaneously over CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr under visible light irradiation; (b) XRD pattern of CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr photocatalyst before and after repeated run. The inset was the TEM image of CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr after four cycles.

(a) The full XPS spectra of the used CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr in simultaneous photocatalytic redox conversion of TCH and Cr(VI); (b) High resolution XPS spectra of Cr 2p for the used CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr; (c) Degradation pathway of TCH in simultaneous photocatalytic redox conversion of TCH and Cr(VI) over CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr.
Acute toxicity LC50 of (a) fathead minnow and (b) daphnia magna; (c) acute toxicity LD50 of oral rat; (d) mutagenicity of TCH and its degradation products.
The photocatalysis enhanced mechanism of CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr sample in the TCH + Cr(VI) co-existence system.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots induce in-situ formation of oxygen vacancies and domination of {001} facets in BiOBr microflower for simultaneous removal of aqueous tetracycline and hexavalent chromium

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Groundwater Resources and Environment, Ministry of Education, Jilin University, Changchun 130021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Groundwater Resources and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Lab of Groundwater Resources and Environment, Ministry of Education, Jilin University, Changchun 130021, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jilin Provincial Key Laboratory of Water Resources and Environment, Jilin University, Changchun 130021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jilin Provincial Key Laboratory of Water Resources and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jilin Provincial Key Laboratory of Water Resources and Environment, Jilin University, Changchun 130021, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Research Institute, Jilin University, Chongqing, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Research Institute, Jilin University, Chongqing, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Lab of Groundwater Resources and Environment, Ministry of Education, Jilin University, Changchun 130021, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Groundwater Resources and Environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Crystal facet engineering and defect engineering are promising strategies to enhance the photocatalytic performance of BiOBr, but limited work has been reported to realize the simultaneous exposure of highly active facets and the introduction of oxygen vacancies by a single approach. In this work, carbon quantum dots-modified bismuth oxybromide (CQDs/BiOBr) with {001} exposed facets and surface oxygen vacancies was synthesized by a two-step hydrothermal method. The characterization and density functional theory calculation results verified that the introduction of CQDs promoted the growth of BiOBr with highly exposed {001} crystal facets and induced the in situ formation of oxygen vacancies by extracting O atoms from the [Bi2O2]2+ layer. The dual functionality of the CQDs allowed them to simultaneously remove tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) and Cr(VI) under visible light irradiation. The photocatalytic reaction rate constants in the coexisting pollutant system were 3.71 (TCH) and 13.6-times (Cr(VI)) higher than those of the mono-pollutant systems. This work provides a candidate method to design highly efficient catalysts for environmental remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past few decades, the ever-increasing discharge of industrial wastewater and pharmaceutical wastewater has caused serious water pollution [1]. There are multiple pollutants in water. Contaminants such as heavy metals, antibiotics, and pathogenic bacteria have severely harmed both humans and the ecological environment [2–4]. Particularly, the degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) and the reduction of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) have drawn great attention due to their high stability and poor biodegradability [5,6]. Cr(VI) is usually discharged along with organic pollutants, which makes water treatment more challenging [7–9]. A series of technologies have been applied to water purification, such as biodegradation, physical adsorption, electrolysis and photocatalytic technology [10–12]. Among these methods, photocatalysis is considered as an advanced technology to remove organic pollutants and heavy metals from water due to its unique advantages of green, economic and high efficiency [13,14]. However, the vast majority of research has only focused on either the degradation of organic pollutants or the reduction of Cr(VI) via photocatalysis [15–17]. The utilization of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in photocatalytic processes is unsatisfactory, which wastes unreacted carriers and also was unfavorable for charge carrier separation. Therefore, the simultaneous degradation of TCH and reduction of Cr (VI) has become a great challenge. There is an urgent need to develop a dual-function photocatalyst for the simultaneous removal of coexisting pollutants in wastewater.

Bi-based photocatalysts exhibit high photocatalytic efficiency. Among the various bismuth-based materials, BiOBr is especially attractive due to its suitable band gap of ∼ 2.6 eV and stable chemical properties [18]. In previous reports, BiOBr was employed to eliminate organic pollutants and heavy metal ions, but bulk BiOBr materials often have a low carrier migration rate and rapid carrier recombination [19]. Previously, crystal facet engineering and the introduction of oxygen vacancies (OVs) were used to boost the photocatalytic activity of BiOBr [20]. Specifically, the {001} facet of BiOBr could separate photoinduced carriers more efficiently due to the clear boundary between [Bi2O2] + and halogen slabs [19]. The (001) facet of BiOX is terminated with a high density of oxygen atoms, which can produce oxygen defects with the presence of ethylene glycol or PVP in synthesis system [21,22]. Oxygen vacancies are crystal defects that enhance the photocatalytic properties of BiOBr by accelerating charge carrier separation and introducing more active sites [23].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials that have been employed to modify semiconductors to promote photocatalytic oxidation or reduction activity due to its excellent light response and electrical conductivity [24–26]. CQDs also affect crystal facets, and similar results have been found in CQDs/BiOBr and CQDs/BiOCl systems [27,28]. Nevertheless, the transformation mechanism of crystal facets after introducing CQDs is ambiguous. Conducting polymers such as polyaniline (PANI) [29] and polypyrrole (PPy) [30] have been reported to be responsible for the in situ generation of oxygen vacancies. The strong interactions between a conducting polymer and semiconductor promotes the formation of oxygen vacancies on the surface during the synthesis of a catalyst. With this interaction, the Bi–O bond extended, resulting in the breaking of the Bi–O bond. Finally, the O atom was extracted and the oxygen vacancies was formed. CQDs may be used to introduce OVs given that their physicochemical properties are similar to those of PANI and PPy [29,30]. Yu et al. [24] synchronously loaded N-doped CQDs and OVs on the surface of BiOBr, and the composites exhibited better photodegradation of TCH than BiOBr modified by only OV or N-CQDs. However, neither the role of crystal facets nor the photoreduction ability was studied. To the best of our knowledge, limited work has been documented on controlling the {001} facet growth and OV generation of BiOBr photocatalysts by single CQDs modification.

In this work, we introduced CQDs to achieve the dominant {001} facets in BiOBr and formed oxygen vacancies on this facet via a hydrothermal method. The as-prepared catalysts (CQDs/BiOBr) realized the simultaneous removal of tetracycline and Cr(VI) under visible light irradiation. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations and various characterization methods were carried out to investigate the role of the CQDs in the formation of oxygen vacancies and the growth of the dominant {001} crystal facet. This strategy of synchronously forming OVs and the dominant crystal facet provides a strategy for the design of novel BiOBr-based photocatalysts.

A reported hydrothermal method was used to synthesize CQDs [31]. 2 mmol Bi (NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved in 20 mL ethanol with vigorous stirring for 30 min to form a homogeneous suspension solution, labelled as solution A. Meanwhile, 2 mmol cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide and a certain amount of CQDs were dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol with vigorous stirring for 30 min to obtain solution B. The solution A was added into solution B under magnetic stirring for another 30 min. The mixture was transfer into 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, which was then heated at 160 °C for 24 h. After the autoclave was naturally cooled down to room temperature, the resulting precipitates were collected by centrifugation, washed with distilled water and ethanol for several times, and then dried at 80 °C for 12 h. Additionally, mass ratio of CQDs to BiOBr was 0.5%, 1.0%, 1.5% and 2.5%, respectively. The corresponding samples were labeled as CQDs(x)/BiOBr (x = 0.5%, 1.0%, 1.5% and 2.5% respectively).

The crystalline phase and structures of the as-prepared samples were characterized by powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8, Germany) using Cu Kα radiation. The oxygen defects were detected by electron spin resonance (EPR) measurement (Bruker, Germany) at liquid nitrogen temperature. The chemical states were performed via a VG Multilab 2000 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) with Al Kαr addition (h = 1486.6 eV). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were obtained by a Spectrum One (Perkin Elmer). Raman spectroscopic measurements were carried out by a Renishaw Invia Raman System with a 532 nm excitation source. The morphologies of the catalysts were conducted by JSM-6700F scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and 200 kV FEI-Tecnai F20 Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). Scanning TEM elemental mapping were performed at 200 kV, using a JEOL JEM-2100F system. The element compositions and mapping of the materials were characterized by an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) attached to the STEM. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of the samples were obtained by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 750, Perking Elmer) using BaSO4 as reference. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of photocatalysts were carried out on a Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Varian Medical Systems, USA). The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were conducted by a Bruker model ESR JES-FA200 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) using DMPO as the spin trapper.

The photocatalytic activity of CQDs(x)/BiOBr was examined by simultaneous reduction of Cr(VI) and degradation TCH undea 150 W Xenon lamp with 400 nm filter (CEL-HXF 300, Beijing Education Au-light Co., Ltd.). Typically, 0.01 g photocatalyst was dispersed into 50 mL aqueous solution, which contained 20 mg/L TCH and 10 mg/L Cr(VI). The above mixture was stirring for 60 min to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium in dark. After irradiation, 3 mL of the samples were taken from the photocatalytic reaction suspension at 10 min interval, followed by filtration through 0.22 μm Millipore filter to remove the photocatalyst. The TCH concentration was analyzed by an Agilent 1100 HPLC with 4.6 × 250 mm, 5 μm Venusil HILIC column and UV detector (λ = 356 nm). 0.02 M acetic acid /acetonitrile (90:10, v/v) was used as the mobile phase at a flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1. The Cr(VI) concentration during the reaction was calorimetrically monitored at 540 nm using the standard diphenylcarbazide method.

Radical trapping experiments were carried out to investigate the main active species by employed tertiary butanol (t-BuOH, 0.1 mM), ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na, 0.1 mM), potassium bromate (PB, 0.1 mM) and 4-benzoquinone (BQ, 0.1 mM) as the scavenger of •OH, h+, e− and •O2 −, respectively.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and transient photocurrent responses of the as-prepared samples were performed on an electrochemical workstation with a three-electrode system (CHI660E, Chenhua Instruments Co. Shanghai, China) using a three-electrode configuration. The working electrode was the FTO glass coated by 5 mg of samples. A150 W Xenon lamp with a cutoff filter (780 nm > λ > 420 nm) was used as light source. The test was measured in 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution.

The XRD patterns in Fig. 1 a show that all as-prepared samples exhibited strong diffraction peaks corresponding to pristine tetragonal-phase BiOBr (JCPDS 09–0393). The characteristic peaks at 10.9°, 21.9°, 25.2°, 31.7°, 32.2°, and 46.3° were ascribed to the (001), (002), (101), (012), (110), and (020) planes, respectively. The CQDs(x)/BiOBr composites exhibited a gradual increase in the (110) diffraction peak intensity upon increasing the CQDs content, which revealed the increased exposure of the {001} facet of BiOBr. As shown in Table S1, the intensity ratio of (110) to (001) diffraction peaks for all samples gradually ascended with the increase of CQDs content, indicating that the BiOBr was oriented along the [001] crystallographic direction after CQDs modification. This phenomenon suggested that the presence of CQDs influenced the growth of the BiOBr crystal. No diffraction peaks of carbon quantum dots were observed at 26.0° due to their low content. The EPR results (Fig. 1b) revealed that the pristine BiOBr possessed a negligible oxygen vacancy signal. The obvious signals appeared at g = 2.003 for all the CQDs decorated samples, while the EPR intensity increased with the increasing in CQDs content. This indicated that oxygen vacancies were fabricated in CQDs(x)/BiOBr [30].

Previous work has reported that oxygen vacancies can be created via the strong interactions at the interface between the photocatalyst (e.g., BiOCl, Bi2O2CO3) and conducting polymer, which was ascribed to hydrogen-bond interactions that broke the Bi–O bond [29,30]. On account of this, CQDs(x)/BiOBr was expected to modify the surface of BiOBr with oxygen vacancies.

The periodic calculations using first-principles theory were carried out to examine the relationship between the introduction of CQDs and the generation of OVs. As presented in Fig. S1, the O atom on the {001} facet surface was more exposed than on other facets. Therefore, CQDs tended to adsorb on the {001} facet surface due to the hydrogen-bond between the BiOBr and CQDs. Additionally, upon increasing the deposition content of CQDs onto the {001} surface of BiOBr, the formation energy of OVs decreased (Fig. 1c). These results suggest that the {001} facet gradually became the dominant facet, and OVs formed on the BiOBr surface. Meanwhile, the Bi–O bond length lengthened from 2.347 Å to 2.805 Å due to enhanced interactions between the CQDs and BiOBr, which indicates the breaking of the Bi–O bond. Consequently, O atoms were extracted from the [Bi2O2]2+ layer, leading to the formation of OVs on the {001} facet (Fig. 1d); therefore, as expected from theoretical calculations, oxygen vacancies were successfully fabricated.

As shown in Fig. 2 , high-resolution XPS was implemented to confirm the surface chemical states and interfacial interactions of BiOBr and CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr. The full-scan XPS spectrum of CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr (Fig. 2a) showed the existence of Bi, O, Br, and C elements in the composite. Fig. 2b depicted the Bi 4f XPS spectra of BiOBr and CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr. The binding energies of Bi 4f 7/2 and Bi 4f 5/2 were 159.4 eV and 164.7 eV, respectively, which were assigned to Bi3+ [32]. As shown in Fig. 2c, the O 1 s XPS spectrum of CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr could be deconvoluted into four peaks at 529.9, 531.0, 531.8, and 533.1 eV, corresponding to the Bi–O, oxygen vacancies (OVs) of BiOBr, C = O bond in CQDs and oxygen-containing functional groups adsorbed on the surface of BiOBr, respectively [28,33]. The presence of oxygen vacancies in CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr was ascribed to the introduction of CQDs. Similarly, the O1s spectrum of BiOBr could be deconvoluted into two peaks at 529.7 and 531.5 eV, corresponding to Bi–O and OH−. The XPS spectrum of Br 3d in Fig. 2d confirmed the existence of Br− [34]. Moreover, the Bi 4f, O 1 s, and Br 1 s peaks of CQDs (1.5%)/BiOBr shifted slightly to higher binding energy, while their intensity decreased dramatically compared to those of pristine BiOBr. Consequently, compared with BiOBr, the binding energies of both O and Br atoms in CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr shifted positively, indicating the presence of strong interactions between BiOBr and CQDs [27]. This resulted in an alternating distribution of electric charges of O and Br atoms in the composite. As for Fig. 2e, the C 1 s spectra of CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr could be resolved into three peaks at 288.4, 284.7, and 286.0 eV, which were identified as sp 2 hybridized C atoms in carbon quantum dots (C–C), C − C/C = C, and C = O, respectively [31,35]. The three C 1 s peaks corresponded to the surface functional groups of the CQDs, suggesting that CQDs were deposited on the surface of BiOBr.

The evidence for the successful fabrication of CQDs/BiOBr was investigated via FTIR spectroscopy. Similar characteristic peaks were observed in the spectra of BiOBr and CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr (Fig. 2f). The strong peak at 517 cm−1 was attributed to the Bi − O [36] stretching mode in BiOBr and CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr materials. The absorption peaks at 1462, 1946 and 1618 cm−1 were assigned to the –COO–, C = O and C–O bonds, respectively, for the CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr materials[37,38]. Moreover, the band at 2922 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–H [39], which revealed the existence of CQDs; thus, the FTIR spectra confirmed that the CQDs/BiOBr composites were successfully fabricated, in accordance with the XPS analysis.

The Raman spectra were obtained to further compare the surfaces of the pristine BiOBr and CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr samples. As shown in Fig. S2, the D band and G band were found in the CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr spectrum, which implies the presence of CQDs[40].

As presented in Fig. 3 a and b, the SEM images showed that both BiOBr and CQDs (1.5%)/BiOBr were composed of nanosheets, which were assembled to form a hierarchical microstructure. In addition, the hierarchical microflower of CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr presented nanosheets with smaller size and tighter together, which were beneficial to high active site distribution [41] and high photocatalytic performance. Similar to the CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr, the composites with other CQDs loading amount were also composed of nanosheets, and the nanosheets of all CQDs(x)/BiOBr hierarchical microflower were more compact than that of BiOBr (Fig. S3).

The TEM images of CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr showed that nanosheets can be found from the edge of microflower structure (Fig. S4), which also demonstrated that CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr microflower consisted of nanosheets. Fig. 3c showed that CQDs with a diameter of ∼ 5 nm were uniformly dispersed on the surface of the BiOBr and the inset displayed the HETEM image of CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr. The interplanar lattice spacings of 0.321 nm assigned to the (002) plane of CQDs [42], while the 0.277 nm corresponded to (110) plane of BiOBr, which were perpendicular to the (001) planes [43]. The corresponding SAED pattern (Fig. 3d) shows the (020) and (110) planes at a cross-angle of 45°, which was in accordance with the theoretical values. Thus, the set of diffraction spots could be indexed as the [001] zone axis, and the {001} facet was the dominant exposed facet of the highly crystalline CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr. The above results reveal that the CQDs controlled the crystal growth of BiOBr, which was ascribed to the interactions between CQDs and BiOBr. In addition, STEM-EDS elemental mapping measurement was performed to determine the distributions of the Bi, Br, N, C and O elements in CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr. As shown in Fig. S5, all elements could be clearly observed in the figure, which also proved the successful preparation of CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr materials.

The optical properties of CQDs(x)/BiOBr with different amounts of carbon quantum dots were detected by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. As demonstrated in Fig. 4 a, the absorption edges of pristine BiOBr were found near 432 nm. Upon increasing the CQDs content, the absorption edges of the CQDs(x)/BiOBr exhibited a red-shift compared with pristine BiOBr, which was ascribed to the up-conversion photoluminescence of the CQDs [24]. Meanwhile, the absorption intensity in the visible region of the as-prepared samples strengthened gradually. These results suggest that CQDs played a crucial role in utilizing visible light, resulting in the generation of more photoexcited electron-hole pairs. Notably, the color of pristine BiOBr changed from white to faint yellow after the introduction of CQDs, as displayed in Fig. S6. This phenomenon further confirmed the existence of CQDs. As presented in Fig. 4a inset, the band gaps of CQDs(x)/BiOBr (x = 0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2.5%) were determined to be 2.97, 2.77, 2.62, 2.39, and 2.36 eV, respectively, based on the Kubelka-Munk formula [44]. The gradually decreasing band gap upon increasing the CQDs content confirmed that coupling CQDs with BiOBr considerably increased the visible light utilization.

Photoluminescence (PL) analysis and transient photocurrent responses of as-prepared samples were performed to study the transfer and recombination processes of electron-hole pairs in BiOBr and CQDs/BiOBr. As shown in Fig. 4b, the pristine BiOBr showed one strong peak near 532 nm under excitation at 380 nm, which was ascribed to the recombination of electrons and holes. Nevertheless, the PL intensity of CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr was significantly lower than that of pristine BiOBr. This result suggested that the CQDs inhibited carrier recombination in BiOBr [45].

Transient photocurrent responses of different samples were used to describe the photo-generated carrier density. As shown in Fig. 4c, all samples exhibited the regular photogenerated current for five on–off cycles under visible light irradiation, whereas the introduction of CQDs dramatically promoted the photocurrent of BiOBr. The average photocurrent density of CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr increased to ∼1.0 µA cm−2, which was approximately 1.4 times higher than that of BiOBr (∼0.77 µA cm−2). The optical absorption of the CQDs(x)/BiOBr samples in visible light region was enhanced, generating more photo-induced electron. Meanwhile, the generated OVs in CQDs(x)/BiOBr further promoted the electron separation. Therefore, transient photocurrent responses increased with the introduction of CQDs, which further demonstrated that CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr was superior to pristine BiOBr in terms of electron-hole separation efficiency.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was also carried out to investigate the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, where a smaller semicircle radius indicates a lower interfacial charge transport resistance. Fig. 4d displays the Nyquist plots of all samples, where CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr showed the smallest radius [46]. The equivalent electrical circuit was further included in Fig. 4d and Table S2. Compared with other samples, CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr exhibited the lowest charge transfer resistance (603 Ω), which was only around one fifth of that for BiOBr (2764 Ω). The EIS results demonstrate that the formation of CQDs/BiOBr composites facilitated the charge transport process, corresponding to the PL analysis.

The results of radical trapping experiments are demonstrated in Fig. 5 a. When 0.1 mM BQ and 0.1 mM EDTA-2Na were added to the reaction liquor, the removal efficiency of TCH decreased from 85% to 67% (P < 0.01) and 39% (P < 0.01), respectively. Moreover, there was a negligible variation in the photocatalytic activity in the presence of t-BuOH and PB. Combined with the above results, it was concluded that h+ and •O2 − were the dominant active species in the photocatalytic reaction over CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr, while •OH hardly contributed to TCH degradation. Meanwhile, The Cr(VI) reduction efficicey was promoted as EDTA-2Na (P < 0.05) or PB was added (P < 0.01). Thus, e− was the only active species involved in Cr(VI) reduction.

As shown in Fig. 5b, no DMPO-•O2 − adduct signal could be detected for either BiOBr or CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr in the dark. After 5 min visible light illumination, four typical peaks with an intensity ratio of 1: 1: 1: 1 were found for BiOBr, which corresponded to the •O2 − signal. Notably, the relative intensity of these peaks for CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr was much stronger than that of pristine BiOBr, indicative of the higher concentration of •O2 −. Both oxygen vacancies and CQDs could act as electron reservoirs that could trap electrons excited from the photocatalyst by visible light illumination. It inhibited the carrier recombination and was conducive to the generation of •O2 −. Thus, given the coexistence of oxygen vacancies and CQDs, the photocatalytic process over CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr exhibited a much higher •O2 − level than pristine BiOBr. There was no DMPO-•OH adduct signal for either BiOBr or CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr (Fig. S7), which was attributed to the more negative valence band (VB) position of BiOBr (E VB = 1.71 eV, Fig. S8) than •OH/OH−(+2.33 eV vs. NHE) [47]. This suggests that •O2 − was the main reactive species in the photocatalytic process over CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic reaction under single TCH or Cr(VI) system
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activities of the different samples were examined by respectively removing TCH and Cr(VI) under visible light irradiation. As depicted in Fig. 6 a and b, the pristine BiOBr had little adsorption or removal effect on TCH or Cr(VI). However, the introduction of CQDs to BiOBr facilitated the dark adsorption of TCH and Cr(VI) by CQDs(x)/BiOBr, because CQDs could improve the adsorption performance of pollutants due to the presence of hydroxyl groups on CQDs surface [48]. As shown in Fig.S9a, CQD was not the main catalyst for photocatalytic reaction. The photocatalytic activities of all CQDs(x)/BiOBr composites were higher than those of pristine BiOBr. Among all as-prepared samples, CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr exhibited the highest TCH degradation efficiency of 66% after 60 min, which was 21% higher than BiOBr under the same conditions. The pseudo-first-order reaction rate constant of CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr was calculated to be 0.0139 min−1, which was 1.48 times higher than that of pristine BiOBr at 0.0094 min−1 (Fig. S9b). Notably, as the amount of CQDs further increased, the degradation efficiency of TCH decreased because excess CQDs might reduce the number of exposed active sites on the BiOBr surface.

The reduction performance of different photocatalysts was evaluated by reducing Cr(VI) under visible light irradiation (Fig. 6b). Generally, a similar trend with the oxidation performance was observed for the photoreduction of Cr(VI). The Cr(VI) removal efficiency over BiOBr and CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr under visible light irradiation in 60 min was about 9% and 44%, respectively. The corresponding first-order kinetic reaction rate constants were determined to be 0.0001 min−1, and 0.0052 min−1 (Fig. S9c). The above results confirmed that the introduction of CQDs improved the photocatalytic efficiency of BiOBr.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic reaction under coexistence of TCH + Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared CQDs(x)/BiOBr samples was further examined by the simultaneous removal of TCH and reduction of Cr(VI) under visible light irradiation. The concentration of TCH and Cr(VI) remained nearly unchanged under visible light irradiation for 60 min in the coexisting pollutant system in the absence of a photocatalyst, which indicated that the self-photolysis of TCH and Cr(VI) was negligible. Compared with the mono-pollutant systems, the TCH degradation efficiency and Cr(VI) reduction efficiency were enhanced for all CQDs(x)/BiOBr samples at (un)-adjusted pH in the TCH + Cr(VI) coexisting system (Fig. 6c-d). Taking CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr as an example, the TCH removal efficiency of BiOBr and CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr under visible light irradiation in 60 min were 28% and 90%, and the corresponding removal efficiency of Cr(VI) was 23% and 53%, respectively. The kinetic constants of the coexisting pollutant system of TCH and Cr(VI) were calculated to be 0.0297 and 0.0081 min−1 (Fig. S9d-e), which were improved by 1.5-fold and 1.2-fold compared with the mono-pollutant system. The higher removal efficiency of the coexisting system was because TCH and Cr(VI) functioned as scavengers of photoinduced holes and electrons, respectively. This improved the separation and utilization of active carriers during the photoreaction, which further proved that CQDs played a significant role in promoting the photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation.

To investigate the effect of pH on the reduction efficiency of Cr(VI), photocatalytic reduction experiments were performed in a solution with pH = 3. Fig. S9f-i shows that the reduction efficiency of Cr(VI) increased by 8%, and the degradation of TCH did not substantially change for CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr when the pH was adjusted to 3 in the mono-pollutant systems. ∼ 100% removal of Cr(VI) was achieved in the bi-pollutant system within 40 min (Fig. 6f), and the corresponding first-order kinetic constant was determined to be 0.0950 min−1 (Fig. S9k), which was 13.6-fold higher than that in the mono-pollutant system (0.0070 min−1, Fig. S9i). Based on the above results, there were synergistic effects during the simultaneous photocatalytic removal of TCH and Cr(VI) over CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr under visible light irradiation.

CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr was used for four cycles in the coexisting system to investigate its stability during the photocatalytic process (Fig. 7 a). The stability was further tested by XRD and TEM of the CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr sample after four cycles (Fig. 7b). The results showed that there was no significant change in the photocatalytic performance, crystallinity, or morphology after four cycles, suggesting that the sample possessed good physical and chemical stability.

To clarify the degradation pathway of TCH and the reduction products of Cr(VI) over CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr in the coexisting system, LC-MS and XPS were used to detect the reaction solution and photocatalysts, respectively. The full-scan survey XPS spectrum of CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr after the reaction in Fig. 8 a confirmed the presence of Cr atoms on the surface of CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr, in contrast to the fresh sample (Fig. 2a). Cr 2p peaks were found at 577.3 eV and 586.6 eV, which were assigned to Cr(III) [49]. No Cr(VI) peaks were found (Fig. 8b), indicating the complete reduction of Cr(VI). Based on the LC-MS analysis of the solution (Fig. S10), a possible photocatalytic degradation pathway of TCH was proposed in Fig. 8c. The TCH molecule was attacked by •OH to obtain products A (m/z = 477) and B (m/z = 461), and then a dehydration reaction occurred to form product C (m/z = 414). TCH was first transformed into product G (m/z 427) via dehydration. As the photocatalytic reaction proceeded, the products D, E, F, H, I, J, K, and L with m/z values of 321, 283, 245, 390, 367, 321, 245, and 222, respectively, began to form due to decarboxylation, hydroxyl oxidation, and cleavage of the carbon bonds. Finally, these products were further degraded to CO2 and H2O or other small molecules.

The acute toxicity and mutagenicity of TCH and its degradation products were predicted by QSAR using the Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T.). As displayed in Fig. 9 , the LC 50 of fathead minnow, the LC 50 of Daphnia magna, and the LD 50 of rats for TCH were classified as “very toxic”, “harmful”, and “toxic”, respectively. The LC 50 values of fathead minnow, Daphnia magna, and the LD 50 values of rats for most of TCH degradation products were higher than that of the mother pollutant, suggesting that the acute toxicity of degradation products was reduced. Furthermore, TCH is “mutagenic positive” (Fig. 10 d), and the mutagenicity values of products C, E, F, G, K, M, N, and O all decreased to “mutagenicity negative” after the photocatalytic reaction. Therefore, it could be summarized that the persistent pollutants were degraded into compounds with lower toxicities.

Combined with the results of the energy band structure, the main active species, and the analysis of photoinduced charge carrier separation, the photocatalytic reaction mechanism for the simultaneous removal of Cr(VI) and of TCH over CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr was proposed. As depicted in Fig. 10, the presence of CQDs resulted in the predominant exposure of the {001} facet and the in situ generation of OVs on the {001} surface in BiOBr due to the strong interfacial hydrogen bonding between the BiOBr {001} surface and CQDs. Moreover, the light-harvesting capacity of CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr was enhanced compared with BiOBr. Under excitation by λ < 432 nm, photogenerated electron-hole pairs were formed in BiOBr. Influenced by the up-conversion of CQDs, the light of λ1 > 432 nm was converted into visible light with λ2 < 432 nm. As a result, photoinduced electron-hole pairs were generated in CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr, where the electrons were injected into CB BiOBr, and holes remained in the VB. Electrons were first transferred to the OVs-induced defect level [22] followed by migration to the CQDs [18,24] for the direct reduction of Cr(VI) and activation of O2 into •O2 − in the TCH + Cr(VI) co-existing system. The holes directly oxidized the adsorbed TCH rather than oxidizing H2O to •OH because the potential for holes (1.71 eV) was more negative than that of •OH/OH− (+2.33 eV vs NHE). The CQDs worked as an electron reservoir to suppress the recombination of photogenerated carriers. Cr(VI) and TCH acted as efficient scavengers of e− and h+, respectively, to further utilize electron-hole pairs; therefore, the removal efficiencies of TCH and Cr(VI) were improved over CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr in the co-existing system.

The simultaneous removal of TCH and Cr(VI) was accomplished by using microflowers CQDs/BiOBr prepared through a hydrothermal method. The characterization results confirmed that introducing CQDs induced the growth of predominantly {001} facets of BiOBr with oxygen vacancies. CQDs/BiOBr exhibited enhanced photocatalytic performance for removing TCH and Cr(VI) compared with pristine BiOBr. Furthermore, the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/BiOBr in the bi-pollutant coexisting system was higher than that in the mono-pollutant systems. This was ascribed to the fact that TCH acted as h+ scavenger to accelerate the separation of photogenerated carriers. Among all developed catalysts, CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr showed the best photocatalytic activity. Under acidic conditions (pH = 3), ∼96% of TCH was removed in 60 min, while almost 100% of Cr(VI) was removed in 50 min using CQDs(1.5%)/BiOBr in the bi-pollutant system. •O2 − and e−-h+ pairs were the main active species during photocatalysis, and the reaction mechanism was proposed. This work provides an efficient strategy for the treatment of mixed wastewater.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52170079, U20A20322), Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing, China (cstc2021jcyj-msxmX1032, cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0954) and the Programme of Introducing Talents of Discipline to Universities (B16020), China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.136249.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The enhanced photoreduction of Cr(VI) to Cr (III) using carbon dots coupled TiO2 mesocrystals

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, Y., Xu, M., Li, H., Ge, H. The enhanced photoreduction of Cr(VI) to Cr (III) using carbon dots coupled TiO2 mesocrystals. Appl. Catal. B-Environ. 226 (2018): 213-219.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multi-component design and in-situ synthesis of visible-light-driven SnO2/g-C3N4/diatomite composite for high-efficient photoreduction of Cr (VI) with the aid of citric acid

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yuan F, Sun Z, Li C. Multi-component design and in-situ synthesis of visible-light-driven SnO2/g-C3N4/diatomite composite for high-efficient photoreduction of Cr (VI) with the aid of citric acid. J. Hazard. Mater. 396 (2020): 122694.
